outcome_contract.why: The current CI setup is fragmented: validation is duplicated across ci.yml, reusable-app.yml, and per-app workflows. The operator wants the simplest possible model.
outcome_contract.intended_outcome_statement: All apps share one validation workflow (ci.yml) on dev/PR and thin per-app deploy workflows on staging/main, with no validation duplication, no workflow_dispatch, and path-gated skip-if-unchanged semantics.

engineering_coverage.applicable: Clear two-layer model: ci.yml validates, per-app deploys
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Same two-layer model but with paths-ignore sync burden
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Option A is simpler mental model

engineering_coverage.applicable: All pushes get security audit, secret scanning, license checks. workflow_dispatch removed (with auto-pr.yml exception).
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Pure app-only pushes skip security audit/license checks — a governance gap.
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Option A has better security coverage

engineering_coverage.applicable: One validation workflow + one deploy workflow per app. Clear audit trail.
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Same structure but paths-ignore can cause confusion about why ci.yml didn't run for a commit.

engineering_coverage.applicable: turbo --affected handles all validation scoping. App-specific gates (prime firebase, reception firebase rules) move to ci.yml as conditional steps.
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Same validation approach but paths-ignore means some pushes never trigger ci.yml at all.
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Option A — ci.yml is truly universal

engineering_coverage.applicable: merge-gate scopeWorkflowMap + auto-pr.yml dispatch updated. filter-config.ts paths-ignore entries removed.
~6-8 governance jobs run on every push (~5-10 min overhead for pure app-only changes). turbo --affected keeps validation scoped.
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Pure app-only pushes skip governance jobs. Shared package pushes trigger both ci.yml and deploy workflows.
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Option A trades modest CI minutes for simplicity

engineering_coverage.applicable: Phased: update ci.yml + one app, verify, extend. Rollback via git revert.

stage_payload.decision_summary: Choose the simplest architecture for splitting validation (lint/typecheck/test) from deployment across all apps. The operator wants one mental model: ci.yml validates changed code on dev/PR, per-app workflows deploy on staging/main, nothing else. The prior Brikette-only attempt introduced duplication instead of removing it; this analysis covers all apps.
goals: ci.yml is the sole validation workflow for all apps on dev/PR.
Per-app deploy workflows are thin: detect changes, build, deploy. No lint/typecheck/test.
"Nothing changed, nothing to do" at both layers.
Remove workflow_dispatch from app workflows (accounting for auto-pr.yml dependency).
Add staging deploy to apps that lack it.
non_goals: Redesign app code, static-export architecture, or Cloudflare project structure.
Change the dev → staging → main branch promotion policy.
Modify non-app workflows (storybook, consent-analytics, lighthouse, cypress).
constraints: merge-gate.yml watches specific workflow file IDs — must be updated atomically.
auto-pr.yml dispatches brikette.yml by workflow_id — must be accounted for.
Per-app deploy secrets, health checks, and build quirks (route-hide/restore, SOPS decrypt) must be preserved.
Worker apps (caryina, reception, business-os) lack staging wrangler config — adding staging deploy requires wrangler config changes.
assumptions: turbo --affected covers all app workspaces for lint/typecheck/test.
ci.yml governance jobs (security audit, secret scanning, dep alignment, license check, docs lint, token validation) are repo-level checks that should run on all pushes.

evaluation_criteria.Why it matters: Operator's explicit top priority — "simplest, most straightforward"
merge-gate is the PR gating mechanism — breakage blocks all merges
Build quirks, secrets, health checks must survive
Governance jobs running unnecessarily wastes minutes
Fewer files to maintain, less sync burden

options_considered.Option: A: Remove paths-ignore + thin per-app deploy
options_considered.Description: Remove ci.yml paths-ignore entirely. ci.yml validates all pushes. Per-app workflows become deploy-only on staging/main.
options_considered.Upside: Simplest config. No validation duplication. No paths-ignore sync burden. One mental model.
options_considered.Downside: Governance jobs (security audit, secret scanning, dep alignment, license, docs lint, token validation) run on every push including pure app-only changes. ~6-8 extra jobs per push.
options_considered.Key risks: CI minutes increase modestly for pure app-only changes.

options_considered.Option: B: Keep paths-ignore + thin per-app deploy
options_considered.Description: Keep ci.yml paths-ignore for apps with deploy workflows. Per-app deploy workflows are deploy-only.
options_considered.Upside: Saves governance job runs on pure app-only pushes.
options_considered.Downside: Must maintain paths-ignore in sync with app dependency trees. Pure app-only changes get NO governance checks (security audit, license, etc.) — a real coverage gap. If a brikette-only change introduces a license-incompatible dependency, it's missed.
options_considered.Key risks: Governance coverage gap for excluded apps. paths-ignore sync burden remains.

options_considered.Description: Replace reusable-app.yml with a thin reusable-deploy.yml. Per-app callers pass build/deploy commands.
options_considered.Upside: Reduces per-app file size. Shared deploy logic in one place.
options_considered.Downside: Adds indirection. Build quirks (route-hide, SOPS decrypt, OpenNext) vary so much per app that the reusable workflow becomes a kitchen sink again.
options_considered.Key risks: Recreates the reusable-app.yml complexity problem in a different form.
options_considered.Viable?: Yes but defeats the simplicity goal

chosen_approach.Recommendation: Option A — Remove ci.yml paths-ignore entirely. ci.yml validates all pushes via turbo --affected. Per-app workflows become deploy-only on staging/main.
chosen_approach.One mental model: ci.yml validates everything, per-app workflows only deploy.

rejected_approaches: **Option B (keep paths-ignore)** — Rejected because it creates a governance coverage gap for excluded apps (security audit, license checks don't run on pure app-only changes) and perpetuates the paths-ignore sync burden that caused the original fragmentation.

**Option C (reusable deploy workflow)** — Rejected because per-app build quirks (route-hide/restore, SOPS decrypt, OpenNext, preflight scripts, language subsets) vary so much that a reusable workflow becomes a kitchen sink — recreating the reusable-app.yml problem. Thin per-app files are simpler despite more files.

validation_implications: actionlint on all modified workflow files.
merge-gate scopeWorkflowMap must reference correct workflow file IDs.
CI run on dev to confirm turbo --affected covers all apps after paths-ignore removal.
sequencing_constraints: ci.yml paths-ignore removal must happen before or atomically with per-app workflow simplification — otherwise removing per-app validation creates a gap.
merge-gate + auto-pr.yml update must be atomic with workflow file identity changes.
Worker app staging deploy (Phase 4) can be deferred if wrangler config changes are complex — it's additive, not blocking.
risks_to_carry_forward: CMS test duration without 4-shard parallelism may be long — monitor and re-add sharding as a ci.yml conditional job if needed.
auto-pr.yml brikette dispatch may be redundant (brikette.yml triggers on dev push anyway) — verify and potentially remove the dispatch.
XA apps (3 apps in 1 workflow) may need special handling — either keep as one deploy workflow or split into 3.
